examples.explanation:
Step 1: n = 56, factors = []. Loop i from 2 to sqrt(56) ≈ 7.48, so up to 7.
Step 2: i = 2, 56 % 2 == 0, so enter while loop: factors append 2 → [2], n = 56 // 2 = 28.
Step 3: 28 % 2 == 0, factors append 2 → [2, 2], n = 28 // 2 = 14.
Step 4: 14 % 2 == 0, factors append 2 → [2, 2, 2], n = 14 // 2 = 7.
Step 5: 7 % 2 != 0, exit while loop. i = 3, 7 % 3 != 0. i = 4, 7 % 4 != 0. i = 5, 7 % 5 != 0. i = 6, 7 % 6 != 0. i = 7, 7 % 7 == 0, enter while loop: factors append 7 → [2, 2, 2, 7], n = 7 // 7 = 1.
Step 6: After loop, n = 1 ≤ 1, so no additional factor. Return [2, 2, 2, 7].

Step 1: n = 17, factors = []. Loop i from 2 to sqrt(17) ≈ 4.12, so up to 4.
Step 2: i = 2, 17 % 2 != 0. i = 3, 17 % 3 != 0. i = 4, 17 % 4 != 0.
Step 3: After loop, n = 17 > 1, so append n to factors → [17]. Return [17].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Trial Division O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 2 to sqrt(n), dividing n by each integer to find prime factors. Use this for small n or when simplicity is prioritized over speed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization via Trial Division
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to factorize any integer n into its prime factors using trial division.
ladders.explanation: The trial division method for prime factorization leverages the fundamental theorem of arithmetic, which states every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely represented as a product of prime numbers. The intuition is that if n has a composite factor, it must have a prime factor less than or equal to its square root. By iterating from 2 to sqrt(n), we systematically test each integer i to see if it divides n. When i divides n, we add i to the list of prime factors and divide n by i repeatedly until i no longer divides n. This ensures we extract all occurrences of that prime factor. After the loop, if n remains greater than 1, it is itself a prime factor (since all smaller primes have been tested). The algorithm runs in O(sqrt(n)) time because we iterate up to sqrt(n), and each division operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) excluding output storage, as we use only a few variables. This approach is straightforward and works well for small to moderate n, but becomes inefficient for large numbers compared to advanced methods like SPF. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the key subroutine of checking divisibility and updating n. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the remaining n after the loop, not using integer division, or incorrectly limiting the loop to n instead of sqrt(n). Real code pattern: Initialize an empty list for factors. For i from 2 to int(sqrt(n)): while n % i == 0: append i to factors, n = n // i. If n > 1: append n to factors. Return factors.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n = 56, factors = []. Loop i from 2 to sqrt(56) ≈ 7.48, so up to 7.
Step 2: i = 2, 56 % 2 == 0, so enter while loop: factors append 2 → [2], n = 56 // 2 = 28.
Step 3: 28 % 2 == 0, factors append 2 → [2, 2], n = 28 // 2 = 14.
Step 4: 14 % 2 == 0, factors append 2 → [2, 2, 2], n = 14 // 2 = 7.
Step 5: 7 % 2 != 0, exit while loop. i = 3, 7 % 3 != 0. i = 4, 7 % 4 != 0. i = 5, 7 % 5 != 0. i = 6, 7 % 6 != 0. i = 7, 7 % 7 == 0, enter while loop: factors append 7 → [2, 2, 2, 7], n = 7 // 7 = 1.
Step 6: After loop, n = 1 ≤ 1, so no additional factor. Return [2, 2, 2, 7].

Step 1: n = 17, factors = []. Loop i from 2 to sqrt(17) ≈ 4.12, so up to 4.
Step 2: i = 2, 17 % 2 != 0. i = 3, 17 % 3 != 0. i = 4, 17 % 4 != 0.
Step 3: After loop, n = 17 > 1, so append n to factors → [17]. Return [17].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisor Check and Factor Extraction
ladders.desc: Check if a number i divides n and repeatedly extract that factor.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation within the trial division loop. The intuition is that if i divides n (i.e., n % i == 0), then i is a prime factor of n, and we need to remove all occurrences of i from n to avoid redundant checks. By using a while loop, we repeatedly divide n by i as long as i divides n, appending i to the factors list each time. This ensures that we fully extract the multiplicity of the prime factor i. For example, if n = 8 and i = 2, we divide 8 by 2 three times to get factors [2, 2, 2] and n becomes 1. Time complexity per call is O(log_i(n)) in the worst case, but amortized over the entire algorithm, it contributes to the overall O(sqrt(n)). Space is O(1) for variables. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is executed for each i in the main loop to build the complete factorization. Common mistakes include using if instead of while, which would only extract one occurrence of the factor, or not updating n correctly. Real code pattern: while n % i == 0: factors.append(i); n = n // i.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n = 18, i = 2. Check 18 % 2 == 0, so enter while loop.
Step 2: Append 2 to factors → [2], n = 18 // 2 = 9.
Step 3: Check 9 % 2 != 0, exit while loop. Return factors=[2], n=9.

Step 1: n = 27, i = 3. Check 27 % 3 == 0, enter while loop.
Step 2: Append 3 to factors → [3], n = 27 // 3 = 9.
Step 3: Check 9 % 3 == 0, append 3 → [3, 3], n = 9 // 3 = 3.
Step 4: Check 3 % 3 == 0, append 3 → [3, 3, 3], n = 3 // 3 = 1.
Step 5: Check 1 % 3 != 0, exit while loop. Return factors=[3, 3, 3], n=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Up to Square Root
ladders.desc: Loop from 2 to sqrt(n) to find prime factors efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of trial division is that any composite number n must have at least one prime factor less than or equal to its square root. This is because if n = a * b, with a ≤ b, then a ≤ sqrt(n) and b ≥ sqrt(n). By iterating i from 2 to floor(sqrt(n)), we guarantee that we test all potential prime factors a. If n has a factor greater than sqrt(n), it will be paired with a factor less than sqrt(n), which we will have already found and removed. After the loop, if n > 1, it means n is a prime number greater than sqrt(original n), and we add it as a factor. This reduces the time complexity from O(n) to O(sqrt(n)), making it feasible for larger n. The intuition is rooted in number theory and optimization: we only need to check up to sqrt(n) because factors come in pairs. Connection to L1: This loop structure repeatedly calls the divisor check sub-routine for each i. Common mistakes include using i <= n instead of i * i <= n, which is inefficient, or not handling the remaining n after the loop. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n)) + 1): [divisor check]. After loop: if n > 1: factors.append(n).

examples.trace:
Step 1: n = 36, sqrt(36) = 6. Loop i from 2 to 6 inclusive.
Step 2: i = 2: check divisibility (as in L1), factors may update.
Step 3: i = 3: check divisibility.
Step 4: i = 4: check, but 4 is composite; its prime factors 2 already handled.
Step 5: i = 5: check, no division.
Step 6: i = 6: check, but 6 is composite; its primes 2 and 3 handled.
Step 7: After loop, n = 1, so no additional factor. Factors collected during checks.

Step 1: n = 29, sqrt(29) ≈ 5.39, so loop i from 2 to 5.
Step 2: i = 2: 29 % 2 != 0.
Step 3: i = 3: 29 % 3 != 0.
Step 4: i = 4: 29 % 4 != 0.
Step 5: i = 5: 29 % 5 != 0.
Step 6: After loop, n = 29 > 1, so add 29 as factor. Factors: [29].

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Modulo Operations
ladders.desc: Perform division and modulo operations to check divisibility and update numbers.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two fundamental arithmetic operations: modulo (%) and integer division (//). The modulo operation n % i returns the remainder when n is divided by i. If n % i == 0, then i divides n exactly, meaning i is a divisor of n. The integer division n // i returns the quotient without remainder, effectively reducing n by removing one factor of i. In trial division, we use these operations in tandem: first check if i divides n using modulo, and if so, update n using integer division to remove that factor. The intuition is that by repeatedly applying n = n // i while n % i == 0, we extract all powers of i from n. This is efficient because it avoids floating-point arithmetic and works with integers. Time complexity per operation is O(1), and space is O(1). Connection to L2: These operations are used within the loop to implement the divisor check. Common mistakes include using regular division / which yields a float, leading to precision errors, or not using integer division when updating n. Real code pattern: if n % i == 0: n = n // i.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute 10 % 2 = 0, so 2 divides 10.
Step 2: Compute 10 // 2 = 5, so new n = 5.

Step 1: Compute 7 % 3 = 1, so 3 does not divide 7.
Step 2: Compute 7 // 3 = 2 (integer division), but since modulo != 0, we don't update n in this context.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through a Range of Integers
ladders.desc: Iterate from a start to an end value using a loop.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a loop, typically a for loop, to iterate through a sequence of integers from a starting point to an ending point. In trial division, we loop from i = 2 to i = floor(sqrt(n)). The intuition is to systematically test each integer in this range as a potential divisor. A for loop is ideal because we know the exact range beforehand. The loop variable i takes each integer value in the range, and for each i, we perform operations like checking divisibility. This is a fundamental programming construct that allows repetitive execution of code. Time complexity depends on the range size; here it's O(sqrt(n)). Space is O(1) for the loop variable. Connection to L3: The loop structure provides the iteration context where division and modulo operations are applied. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using < instead of <=), or not converting sqrt(n) to an integer properly. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n)) + 1): [operations].

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize i = 2.
Step 2: Execute loop body for i=2.
Step 3: Increment i to 3, execute body.
Step 4: Increment i to 4, execute body.
Step 5: Increment i to 5, execute body.
Step 6: i becomes 6, which is > end, so exit loop.

Step 1: i = 1, execute body.
Step 2: i = 2, execute body.
Step 3: i = 3, execute body.
Step 4: i = 4, exit loop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Identify prime numbers as integers greater than 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and itself.
ladders.explanation: Prime numbers are the building blocks of integers. A prime number is defined as an integer greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are primes. Numbers greater than 1 that are not prime are called composite, meaning they have divisors other than 1 and themselves (e.g., 4, 6, 8). The intuition for trial division relies on this concept: we factorize a number by breaking it down into its prime components. Since every composite number can be expressed as a product of primes, by testing divisibility by integers, we are essentially searching for these prime factors. This foundation is crucial because trial division aims to find prime factors, not just any divisors. Understanding primes helps in optimizing the algorithm—for instance, we only need to test up to sqrt(n) because any composite factor will have a prime factor within that range. Connection to L4: This concept underlies why we loop from 2 (the smallest prime) and check divisibility. Common mistakes include considering 1 as prime (it's not, as it has only one divisor) or 0 as prime (it's not defined). Real code pattern to check if a number p is prime: if p < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(p)) + 1): if p % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace:
Step 1: p = 5 > 1, so proceed.
Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to sqrt(5) ≈ 2.24, so up to 2.
Step 3: i = 2: 5 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, so not divisible.
Step 4: No divisors found, so 5 is prime.

Step 1: p = 6 > 1, so proceed.
Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to sqrt(6) ≈ 2.45, so up to 2.
Step 3: i = 2: 6 % 2 = 0, so divisible.
Step 4: Since a divisor other than 1 and 6 is found, 6 is composite.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Sieve-based SPF O(n log log n) precomputation
approaches.summary: Precompute the smallest prime factor (SPF) for all numbers up to n using a modified sieve of Eratosthenes, then factorize by repeatedly dividing by SPF. Ideal for multiple queries or large n.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log log n) for precomputation, O(log n) per query

ladders.title: Full Problem — Fast Factorization Using SPF
ladders.desc: Given n, factorize it into prime factors using precomputed SPF array.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution that combines SPF precomputation with factorization. The intuition is that by knowing the smallest prime factor for every number up to n, we can repeatedly divide n by its SPF to extract all prime factors efficiently. First, we precompute SPF for all numbers up to n using a modified sieve of Eratosthenes. For each prime p, we mark multiples of p where p is smaller than their current SPF. This ensures SPF[x] stores the smallest prime dividing x. Then, to factorize n, we repeatedly divide n by SPF[n] until n becomes 1, collecting all factors. The precomputation takes O(n log log n) time and O(n) space, while each factorization query takes O(log n) time. This approach is ideal for multiple queries or when n is large. Connection to L1: This uses the SPF precomputation from L1 and the factorization loop from L2. Common mistakes include not handling n=1 correctly (should return empty list), or incorrectly updating n during factorization. Real code pattern: def factorize(n): factors = []; while n > 1: p = spf[n]; factors.append(p); n //= p; return factors.

examples.trace: Precompute SPF up to 1: spf[1]=1. Factorize 1: n=1, while loop condition n>1 is false, return empty list [].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — SPF Precomputation Using Sieve
ladders.desc: Given limit N, compute smallest prime factor for all numbers up to N.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine precomputes the SPF array, which is the core data structure enabling fast factorization. The intuition is to adapt the sieve of Eratosthenes: instead of just marking numbers as prime/composite, we store the smallest prime that divides each number. We initialize spf[i]=i for all i≥2 (assuming each number is its own smallest prime initially). Then, for each i from 2 to N, if spf[i]==i (meaning i is prime), we iterate through multiples j=i*i, i*i+i, ... up to N, and if spf[j]==j (i.e., no smaller prime has marked j yet), we set spf[j]=i. This ensures spf[x] holds the smallest prime dividing x. Time complexity is O(N log log N) similar to the standard sieve, and space is O(N). Connection to L0: This precomputation is the first step in the full solution. Common mistakes include starting j from i instead of i*i (which would overwrite spf for smaller multiples unnecessarily), or not checking spf[j]==j before updating. Real code pattern: def compute_spf(N): spf = list(range(N+1)); for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if spf[i]==i: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): if spf[j]==j: spf[j]=i; return spf.

examples.trace: Initialize spf=[0,1]. No loop since i starts at 2>1. Result: spf[0]=0, spf[1]=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Factorization Loop Using SPF
ladders.desc: Given n and precomputed spf array, extract prime factors by repeated division.
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic that uses the SPF array to factorize a single number n. The intuition is that since spf[n] gives the smallest prime dividing n, dividing n by spf[n] reduces n to a smaller number, and we can repeat this process until n becomes 1, collecting all prime factors in order. This works because after dividing by spf[n], the quotient's smallest prime factor will be at least as large as the previous one, ensuring we get all factors. The algorithm: initialize an empty list factors; while n > 1: p = spf[n]; append p to factors; n = n // p; return factors. Time complexity is O(log n) since each division reduces n by at least a factor of 2 (worst-case when n is a power of 2). Space is O(log n) for storing factors. Connection to L0: This is the second step after SPF precomputation. Connection to L1: It relies on the spf array from L1. Common mistakes include infinite loop if spf[1] is not 1 (should handle n=1 separately), or using integer division incorrectly. Real code pattern: def factorize_using_spf(n, spf): factors = []; while n > 1: factors.append(spf[n]); n //= spf[n]; return factors.

examples.input: n=18, spf from L1 example for N=18
n=7, spf from L1 example for N=7
examples.trace: Assume spf[7]=7. n=7, spf[7]=7, factors=[7], n=7//7=1. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sieve of Eratosthenes for Primes
ladders.desc: Given limit N, generate all prime numbers up to N using sieve.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is the foundation for SPF precomputation. The sieve of Eratosthenes efficiently finds primes by iteratively marking multiples of primes as composite. Intuition: Start with a list is_prime of size N+1 initialized to True. Mark 0 and 1 as False. For i from 2 to sqrt(N): if is_prime[i] is True, then for j from i*i to N step i: mark is_prime[j] = False. The remaining True indices are primes. This works because any composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root. Time complexity is O(N log log N), space O(N). Connection to L1: The SPF sieve modifies this by storing the prime factor instead of just True/False. Common mistakes include not optimizing by starting j from i*i (starting from 2*i is less efficient), or not stopping at sqrt(N). Real code pattern: def sieve(N): is_prime = [True]*(N+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; primes = [i for i, val in enumerate(is_prime) if val]; return primes.

examples.trace: Initialize is_prime=[F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T]. i=2 (prime): j=4,6,8,10 → mark F. is_prime=[F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F]. i=3 (prime): j=9 → mark F. is_prime=[F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F]. i=4 (not prime, skip). i=5>sqrt(10)≈3.16, stop. Primes: indices 2,3,5,7.
After sieve: is_prime has True at indices 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Looping
ladders.desc: Perform repeated integer division and collect results in a list.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves dividing an integer by another integer repeatedly until a condition is met, and storing the divisors in a list. Intuition: Many algorithms, including factorization, require breaking down a number by repeated division. For example, to factorize n naively, we might divide by primes until n becomes 1. The key operations are: integer division (// in Python), which gives the quotient without remainder; appending to a list; and loop control (while or for). Time complexity depends on the number of divisions, often O(log n) if dividing by at least 2 each time. Space is O(k) for storing k results. Connection to L2: This is the mechanical part of the factorization loop. Common mistakes include using floating-point division instead of integer division, or not updating the variable correctly in the loop. Real code pattern: def repeated_division(n, divisor): results = []; while n % divisor == 0: results.append(divisor); n //= divisor; return results, n. This example divides n by a fixed divisor while divisible.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, composite numbers, and basic divisibility.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. A composite number has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself. Divisibility: a divides b (a|b) if b % a == 0. The fundamental theorem of arithmetic states every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely factored into primes. Intuition: Factorization breaks a number into its prime building blocks. For example, 12 = 2×2×3. This concept is foundational because SPF-based factorization relies on identifying the smallest prime divisor. Key operations: checking if a number is prime by testing divisibility up to sqrt(n), or finding divisors. Time complexity for naive prime check is O(sqrt(n)). Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This explains why we divide by primes in factorization. Common mistakes include treating 1 as prime (it's not), or not handling edge cases like n=0 or negative numbers. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: 7>1. Check divisibility by i=2 to sqrt(7)≈2.64: i=2, 7%2=1≠0. i=3>2.64, stop. No divisors found, so 7 is prime.
12>1. Check divisibility: i=2, 12%2=0, so 2 divides 12. Thus 12 is composite. Its prime factors are 2 and 3 since 12=2×2×3.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Pollard's Rho Algorithm O(n^(1/4)) expected
approaches.summary: Use a probabilistic algorithm based on cycle detection to find factors, combining with trial division for small primes. Best for very large numbers where deterministic methods are too slow.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Factorize Large Numbers Using Pollard's Rho Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Pollard's Rho algorithm with trial division for small primes to factorize any integer n > 1.
ladders.explanation: Pollard's Rho is a probabilistic algorithm for integer factorization that's particularly effective for composite numbers with small prime factors. The core idea uses Floyd's cycle detection algorithm to find two numbers x and y such that gcd(|x-y|, n) > 1, which reveals a non-trivial factor of n. For small primes (typically < 1000), we use trial division for efficiency. The algorithm recursively factors the discovered factors until all are prime. The intuition: if we generate a pseudo-random sequence modulo n, by the birthday paradox we expect a collision modulo some factor p of n in O(√p) steps, much faster than O(√n) for trial division. We use the function f(x) = (x² + c) mod n to generate the sequence, where c is a random constant. The algorithm has expected time complexity O(n^(1/4)) because we find factors in time proportional to the square root of the smallest prime factor. Space is O(1) as we only store a few variables. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete factorization solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle n=1, not randomizing c when gcd=1, infinite recursion without proper base cases, and not using Miller-Rabin for primality testing (we'll use deterministic bases for 64-bit). Real code pattern: def pollard_rho(n): if n%2==0: return 2; if is_prime(n): return n; while True: c=random; x=y=2; d=1; while d==1: x=f(x); y=f(f(y)); d=gcd(abs(x-y),n); if d==n: break; if d!=1: return d;

examples.trace: Step 1: n=10403, not even. Step 2: Not prime. Step 3: Trial division up to 1000: none. Step 4: Pollard's Rho: c=1, x=y=2. Iteration 1: x=5, y=26, d=gcd(21,10403)=1. Iteration 2: x=26, y=4582 (since 26²+1=677, 677²+1=4582 mod 10403), d=gcd(4556,10403)=1. Iteration 3: x=677, y=1053, d=gcd(376,10403)=1. Iteration 4: x=4582, y=5774, d=gcd(1192,10403)=1. Iteration 5: x=4196, y=83, d=gcd(4113,10403)=101. Found factor 101. Step 5: 10403/101=103, both prime. Output [101, 103].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard's Rho Factor Finding Function
ladders.desc: Implement the core Pollard's Rho algorithm to find a single non-trivial factor of a composite number n.
ladders.explanation: This function takes an integer n (assumed composite and odd) and returns a non-trivial factor of n. It uses Floyd's cycle detection with the function f(x) = (x² + c) mod n, where c is a randomly chosen constant (typically between 1 and n-1). We initialize two pointers x and y to 2, then repeatedly update x = f(x) (one step) and y = f(f(y)) (two steps). At each step, we compute d = gcd(|x-y|, n). If d > 1, we found a factor: if d < n, return d; if d = n, the algorithm failed (we hit a cycle without finding a factor), so we retry with a different c. The intuition: the sequence {x mod p} for a factor p of n will eventually cycle because it's deterministic modulo p. By the birthday paradox, we expect a collision in O(√p) steps. When x ≡ y (mod p) but x ≠ y (mod n), then |x-y| is a multiple of p but not of n, so gcd(|x-y|, n) reveals p. Time: expected O(n^(1/4)) as we need O(√p) steps where p is the smallest prime factor. Space: O(1). Connection: This finds factors for the full factorization. Common mistakes: not handling d=n case, using fixed c, not using absolute value in gcd. Real code pattern: def pollard_rho_factor(n): if n%2==0: return 2; c=random(1,n-1); x=y=2; d=1; while d==1: x=(x*x+c)%n; y=(y*y+c)%n; y=(y*y+c)%n; d=math.gcd(abs(x-y),n); if d==n: return pollard_rho_factor(n); return d;

ladders.title: Core Logic — Floyd's Cycle Detection in Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Use Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm to detect cycles in a sequence modulo n, aiming to find a collision that reveals a factor.
ladders.explanation: Floyd's cycle detection algorithm is used to find a cycle in a sequence generated by a function f. We maintain two pointers: tortoise (x) moves one step at a time (x = f(x)), and hare (y) moves two steps at a time (y = f(f(y))). If there's a cycle, they will eventually meet. In Pollard's Rho, we apply this modulo n but care about collisions modulo a factor p of n. The sequence is defined as f(x) = (x² + c) mod n. The intuition: modulo a factor p, the sequence will eventually repeat because there are only p possible values. By the birthday paradox, we expect a repeat in O(√p) steps. When x ≡ y (mod p) but x ≠ y (mod n), then |x-y| is divisible by p but not n, so gcd(|x-y|, n) gives a non-trivial factor. The algorithm: initialize x=y=2, choose random c. Loop: x = f(x); y = f(f(y)); compute d = gcd(|x-y|, n); if d > 1, found factor. Time: O(√p) expected. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the cycle detection mechanism in Pollard's Rho. Common mistakes: not using absolute value, not updating y correctly with two steps, confusion between mod n and mod p. Real code pattern: while d==1: x=(x*x+c)%n; y=(y*y+c)%n; y=(y*y+c)%n; d=gcd(abs(x-y),n);

examples.input: n=15, c=1, seq until factor found
examples.trace: n=15, c=1, x=2, y=2, d=1. Step 1: x=(4+1)%15=5; y: first step (4+1)%15=5, second step (25+1)%15=11; d=gcd(|5-11|,15)=gcd(6,15)=3. Found factor 3 in 1 iteration.

ladders.title: Building Block — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the principle that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). We repeatedly replace (a, b) with (b, a mod b) until b becomes 0, at which point a is the GCD. For example, gcd(48, 18) = gcd(18, 12) = gcd(12, 6) = gcd(6, 0) = 6. The intuition: any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b, so the set of common divisors remains unchanged. Time: O(log min(a, b)) as the numbers reduce quickly. Space: O(1) for iterative version. Connection: GCD is used in Pollard's Rho to compute gcd(|x-y|, n) to find factors. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (use absolute values), infinite loop if not reducing properly, recursion depth issues for large numbers. Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a);

examples.trace: a=48, b=18. Step 1: b!=0, compute a mod b = 48 mod 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12. Step 2: b!=0, 18 mod 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6. Step 3: b!=0, 12 mod 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0. Step 4: b=0, return a=6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic and Random Number Generation
ladders.desc: Perform modular operations (addition, multiplication) and generate random numbers within a range.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic deals with numbers wrapping around after reaching a modulus n. Key operations: (a + b) mod n = (a mod n + b mod n) mod n; (a * b) mod n = (a mod n * b mod n) mod n. In Pollard's Rho, we use f(x) = (x² + c) mod n. We need to compute this efficiently without overflow by applying mod after each operation. Random number generation: choose a random integer c between 1 and n-1 to seed the function f. The randomness helps avoid bad cases where the sequence degenerates. Use a pseudorandom generator. Intuition: modular arithmetic keeps numbers bounded, and randomness ensures probabilistic success. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection: This computes the next values in the sequence for cycle detection. Common mistakes: overflow in x² (use modular multiplication), not reducing mod n correctly, using poor random seeds. Real code pattern: x = (x * x + c) % n; for random: c = random.randint(1, n-1);

examples.trace: 5² = 25. 25 mod 7 = 4 (since 7*3=21, remainder 4). Then 4 + 3 = 7. 7 mod 7 = 0. Alternatively: (5² + 3) = 28, 28 mod 7 = 0.
Random integer between 1 and 9 inclusive. Possible values: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9. Example output 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Remainders
ladders.desc: Understand integer division (quotient and remainder) and the modulo operation.
ladders.explanation: When dividing an integer a by a positive integer b, we get a quotient q and a remainder r such that a = b * q + r, where 0 ≤ r < b. The remainder r is a mod b. For example, 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3 and remainder 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2. The modulo operation finds this remainder. Key properties: (a mod n) is always between 0 and n-1; if a is negative, different languages handle it differently, but in math, we adjust to keep remainder non-negative. Intuition: division splits a into equal parts of size b, with r left over. This is fundamental for modular arithmetic and GCD. Time: O(1) in hardware. Space: O(1). Connection: Modular arithmetic in Pollard's Rho uses remainders mod n. Common mistakes: confusing quotient and remainder, misunderstanding negative remainders. Real code pattern: q = a // b (integer division), r = a % b (remainder).

examples.trace: 17 = 5 * 3 + 2. So quotient is 3, remainder is 2. 17 mod 5 = 2.
10 = 3 * 3 + 1. Quotient 3, remainder 1. 10 mod 3 = 1.
